key_insight: The paper turns inverse materials design into a differentiable optimization problem by embedding reverse-mode automatic differentiation into KKR-CPA, allowing compositions to be optimized directly toward target properties.
engineering_relevance: This provides a first-principles, gradient-based route to discover candidate alloys and half-metals across large compositional spaces without relying on massive training datasets or exhaustive screening.
method: Treat atomic composition as continuous variables in KKR-CPA.
Use reverse-mode AD to compute composition gradients of any objective at cost independent of candidate count.
Apply gradient-based optimization to two targets: magnetic alloys and half-metals.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports successful demonstrations on two representative design problems, producing example candidates such as (Lu0.553Yb0.447)(Co0.759Fe0.241)2Fe3 and FeZr(Sb0.94Te0.06).
watch_item: The approach depends on differentiable first-principles modeling and coherent-potential approximations, so accuracy and practicality may be limited for strongly disordered, highly correlated, or structurally complex systems.
paper_title: A Differentiable DFT-Based Framework for Inverse Materials Design
